activities_projects.title: Visualizing the Three Estates
activities_projects.objective: Understand the social hierarchy and tax burden in 18th-century France
steps: Review Fig. 2 'A Society of Estates' in the text [cite: 73, 92]
Create a pyramid diagram representing the Clergy, Nobility, and the Third Estate [cite: 84, 86, 88]
Shade the Third Estate differently to show they were the only group paying taxes [cite: 70, 83]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the inequality of the 'Old Regime' social structure [cite: 72, 77]
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct labeling and representation of the three estates (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Decoding Revolutionary Symbols
activities_projects.objective: Learn how symbols were used to communicate ideas to an uneducated population
steps: List five symbols from 'Box 1: Reading political symbols' (e.g., broken chain, red cap, Sceptre) [cite: 365, 373, 377, 384]
Draw each symbol and write its meaning next to it [cite: 369]
Explain why symbols were more effective than books for the majority of people in 1789 [cite: 366, 367]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Translates visual political metaphors into abstract concepts like freedom and unity [cite: 375, 377]
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of symbol meanings and quality of drawings (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Bread Price Crisis Timeline
activities_projects.objective: Trace the economic causes that led to the storming of the Bastille
steps: Identify the population growth figures between 1715 and 1789 [cite: 122]
Create a flow chart showing the link between bad harvests, rising bread prices, and food riots [cite: 124, 127, 138]
Define 'Subsistence Crisis' based on the text [cite: 128, 130]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes how economic hardship triggers political revolt [cite: 48, 142]
activities_projects.evaluation: Logical sequencing of the economic 'chain of events' (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Defining the Rights of Man
activities_projects.objective: Familiarize students with the foundational democratic rights established in 1791
steps: Select four key rights from the 'Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen' (Source C) [cite: 344, 345]
Write these rights in simplified, modern language [cite: 346, 351, 356]
Discuss why the state considered these rights 'natural and inalienable' [cite: 334, 335]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the origin of modern democratic principles [cite: 12, 13, 655]
activities_projects.evaluation: Clear simplification and understanding of the 'natural rights' concept (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Mapping the 'Great Fear'
activities_projects.objective: Understand the spread of the revolution from Paris to the countryside
steps: Refer to Fig. 6: 'The spread of the Great Fear' [cite: 277]
Mark the epicenters of panic and use arrows to show the direction of movement [cite: 275]
Briefly describe what peasants did when they attacked 'chateaux' [cite: 238, 239]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes the geographic scale and intensity of the agrarian revolt [cite: 47, 237]
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of map markings and description of peasant actions (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Active vs. Passive Citizens Comparison
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the limitations of the 1791 Constitution
steps: Create a T-chart comparing 'Active Citizens' and 'Passive Citizens' [cite: 304, 305]
List the criteria for voting (age, gender, tax bracket) [cite: 311, 312]
Explain Marat's criticism regarding the influence of wealth on law (Source B) [cite: 337, 339]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that early revolutionary reforms did not grant equality to everyone [cite: 417, 535]
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct categorization of citizen groups (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: A Day in the Life of a Revolutionary Woman
activities_projects.objective: Explore the role and challenges of women during the revolution
steps: List the typical jobs and daily tasks of women of the Third Estate [cite: 516, 517, 520]
Write a short diary entry from the perspective of a woman marching to Versailles on 5 October 1789 [cite: 512, 513]
Mention one law passed by the revolutionary government to help women [cite: 538, 539, 542]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Appreciates the active participation of women despite limited political rights [cite: 514, 534]
activities_projects.evaluation: Historical accuracy and empathy in the diary entry (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Jacobin Wardrobe
activities_projects.objective: Understand how clothing was used as a political statement
steps: Sketch a 'sans-culottes' couple based on Fig. 9 [cite: 429]
Label the long striped trousers and the red cap [cite: 425, 445]
Explain why they refused to wear 'knee breeches' [cite: 426, 443]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Connects fashion choices to political identity and social class [cite: 423, 444]
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of clothing items and their significance (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Rise and Fall of Robespierre
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the 'Reign of Terror' and its methods
steps: Define 'Guillotine' and explain why it was used [cite: 462, 463]
List three changes Robespierre made to everyday life (e.g., bread, address, churches) [cite: 466, 468, 469]
State why even his supporters eventually turned against him [cite: 470, 471]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the transition from democracy to a period of severe control [cite: 459, 461]
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity in defining terms and listing government measures (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Napoleon’s Legacy Report
activities_projects.objective: Summarize the impact of Napoleon Bonaparte on France and Europe
steps: Note the year Napoleon crowned himself Emperor [cite: 650]
List two modernizing laws he introduced (e.g., weights and measures) [cite: 651]
Discuss why he was initially seen as a 'liberator' and later as an 'invader' [cite: 652, 653]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies Napoleon's role as both a conqueror and a modernizer [cite: 505, 654]
activities_projects.evaluation: Quality of the summary of Napoleon's dual impact (10 marks)
